mission.title: Install and Configure Linkerd Viz (Linkerd) on Kubernetes
mission.description: Linkerd Viz is a powerful observability tool for the Linkerd service mesh, providing insights into the performance and health of your microservices. Installing Linkerd Viz allows you to visualize service interactions, request rates, latencies, and more, enhancing your monitoring capabilities.

steps.title: Add the Linkerd Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Linkerd Helm repository to your Helm client to access the Linkerd charts.
```bash
helm repo add linkerd https://linkerd.github.io/linkerd2-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Linkerd Control Plane
steps.description:
Install the Linkerd control plane using Helm. This is a prerequisite for Linkerd Viz.
```bash
helm install linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --create-namespace --version edge-26.2.1
```

steps.title: Install Linkerd Viz
steps.description:
Now, install Linkerd Viz to enable observability features for your Linkerd service mesh.
```bash
helm install linkerd-viz linkerd/linkerd-viz --namespace linkerd --version edge-26.2.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Linkerd Viz components are running correctly by listing the pods in the Linkerd namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n linkerd
```

steps.title: Access the Linkerd Viz dashboard
steps.description:
To access the Linkerd Viz dashboard, you can port-forward the service to your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward -n linkerd svc/linkerd-viz 8085:8085
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for the Linkerd Viz components. Edit the values.yaml file and set resource requests and limits as needed, then upgrade the release:
```bash
helm upgrade linkerd-viz linkerd/linkerd-viz --namespace linkerd --values values.yaml
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Linkerd Viz will show all components running when you execute `kubectl get pods -n linkerd`. You can access the dashboard by navigating to http://localhost:8085 in your web browser.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add linkerd https://linkerd.github.io/linkerd2-charts
helm repo update
helm install linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --create-namespace --version edge-26.2.1
helm install linkerd-viz linkerd/linkerd-viz --namespace linkerd --version edge-26.2.1

uninstall.title: Remove the Linkerd Viz Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Linkerd Viz installation, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall linkerd-viz --namespace linkerd
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any custom resource definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd --all
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace linkerd
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Linkerd namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the Linkerd namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace linkerd
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of the Linkerd Viz release:
```bash
helm get values linkerd-viz --namespace linkerd > linkerd-viz-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then upgrade the Linkerd Viz release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade linkerd-viz linkerd/linkerd-viz --namespace linkerd --version edge-26.3.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Linkerd Viz components are running correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n linkerd
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that the Linkerd Viz pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n linkerd
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Linkerd Viz dashboard not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Linkerd Viz dashboard, ensure that the port-forward command is running. You can restart it with:
```bash
kubectl port-forward -n linkerd svc/linkerd-viz 8085:8085
``` 
Then, try accessing it again at http://localhost:8085.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod evictions
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being evicted due to resource limits, check the events in the namespace:
```bash
kubectl get events -n linkerd
``` 
Consider adjusting the resource requests and limits in your values.yaml file and upgrading the release.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, ensure that the version specified is available in the Helm repository:
```bash
helm search repo linkerd/linkerd-viz --versions
``` 
Then, specify a valid version in the upgrade command.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
